message:
fix(sqlmodel-sqlite): always bundle libsqlite3 instead of dynamically linking the host sqlite3

Switch sqlmodel-sqlite from host-libsqlite3 linkage to the bundled
amalgamation on every target, and stop declaring an explicit
`#[link(name = "sqlite3")]` attribute in the FFI module.

### What changed

`crates/sqlmodel-sqlite/Cargo.toml`
  - Replace the Windows-only dependency line
      libsqlite3-sys = { workspace = true, features = ["bundled-windows"] }
    with an unconditional
      libsqlite3-sys = { workspace = true, features = ["bundled"] }
    so every build target compiles SQLite from the vendored C
    amalgamation and links it statically.

`crates/sqlmodel-sqlite/src/ffi.rs`
  - Drop the per-target `#[cfg_attr]` link attributes on the
    `extern "C"` block:
      #[cfg_attr(windows, link(name = "sqlite3", kind = "static"))]
      #[cfg_attr(not(windows), link(name = "sqlite3"))]
    Linking is now owned entirely by the `libsqlite3-sys` crate,
    which emits `cargo:rustc-link-lib=static=sqlite3` under the
    `bundled` feature. The extern block resolves against those
    bundled symbols automatically.
  - Add a comment explaining why the explicit link attribute had
    to go away, for the next person who sees it and thinks about
    putting it back.

`Cargo.lock`
  - Regenerated by Cargo after the feature change. Net-negative
    diff as the bundled feature prunes a few transitive
    host-linkage optional deps.

### Why

The previous configuration worked on glibc Linux hosts that
happened to have `libsqlite3-dev` installed — the link step would
bind dynamically to `libsqlite3.so`. Two problems surfaced from
that:

  1. On musl (Alpine, musl cross-builds), there is no host
     `libsqlite3.so` or `.a` to bind against and the build failed
     outright at link time.

  2. On glibc hosts where `libsqlite3-dev` ships a static archive,
     the linker would silently bind against the host
     `libsqlite3.a` that had been compiled with `_FORTIFY_SOURCE`.
     Cross-linking that archive into a musl target then failed
     with unresolved `__memcpy_chk` (a FORTIFY intrinsic that
     musl's libc deliberately does not expose).

Bundling sqlite3 side-steps both — the amalgamation is compiled
inside this crate's build with whatever compiler flags match the
target triple, so there's no hidden dependency on the host's
sqlite packaging, and the result builds cleanly on musl, Alpine,
Windows, macOS, and every gnu Linux.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

@@ -29,8 +29,15 @@ serde_json.workspace = true
 # Optional console support
 sqlmodel-console = { workspace = true, optional = true }
 
-[target.'cfg(windows)'.dependencies]
-libsqlite3-sys = { workspace = true, features = ["bundled-windows"] }
+# libsqlite3-sys with `bundled` compiles SQLite from the vendored amalgamation
+# on every target and emits `cargo:rustc-link-lib=static=sqlite3`. That replaces
+# the previous #[link(name = "sqlite3")] dynamic-link attribute in src/ffi.rs,
+# which refused to work on musl (no host libsqlite3.so/.a) and would silently
+# pick up the host glibc libsqlite3.a on gnu Linux — tripping `__memcpy_chk`
+# unresolved symbols under musl's non-FORTIFY libc. Bundling sqlite3 makes the
+# build work on musl, Alpine, Windows, macOS, and every gnu Linux without any
+# dependency on the host's libsqlite3 package.
+libsqlite3-sys = { workspace = true, features = ["bundled"] }
 
 [build-dependencies]
